name: B2B Cold Outreach
description: A personalized cold email for B2B sales. A prospect planner maps the angle (scratch), then subject and email agents write the final copy.

variables.label: Prospect Company
variables.hint: The company you're reaching out to

variables.label: Pain Point or Challenge
variables.exampleValue: scaling a sales team without losing deal quality
variables.hint: The specific problem this prospect likely has — be concrete

variables.label: What You Offer
variables.exampleValue: a sales coaching platform that cuts ramp time by 40%
variables.hint: Your product or service and its primary benefit

variables.label: Your Name
variables.hint: Your first name — used in the sign-off

variables.label: Social Proof (Optional)
variables.exampleValue: We helped HubSpot reduce ramp time from 6 months to 10 weeks
variables.hint: A specific result or logo name — leave blank if none available

agents.name: Prospect Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan B2B cold outreach emails before any copy is written. Your plan identifies: the specific hook for line 1 (something about them, not you), how pain connects to offer in one sentence, whether social proof fits and where, and the low-commitment CTA angle. This plan is used by the writing agents and does not appear in the sent email.

agents.name: Subject Line Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write cold email subject lines that get opened. The best cold subject lines are short (under 6 words), specific to the recipient or their situation, and create just enough curiosity without being clickbait. They never use 'quick question', 'touching base', or a generic benefit statement. Return only the subject line — no 'Subject:' prefix, no quotes.

agents.name: Email Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write B2B cold emails that get replies. Rules: under 120 words, 3 short paragraphs, one CTA. Structure: line 1 is specific to them (not about you), line 2 connects their pain to your offer in one sentence, line 3 is social proof if available, final line is a low-commitment CTA. No fluffy openers. No 'I hope this finds you well.' Sign with the sender's name only.

name: Follow-Up Email
description: A 3-touch follow-up sequence for a cold email that got no response. A sequence planner maps each touch (scratch), then subject and body agents write the final emails.

variables.label: Original Email Topic
variables.exampleValue: reducing sales ramp time with coaching software
variables.hint: What the first email was about — in a few words

variables.label: New Angle or Value Add
variables.defaultValue: a case study or relevant insight
variables.exampleValue: a 2-minute video walkthrough of our ROI calculator
variables.hint: What new thing you can offer in follow-up 2 — resource, case study, insight

agents.name: Sequence Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan 3-email cold follow-up sequences before any copy is written. Email 1 (Day 3): reframe angle. Email 2 (Day 7): add genuine value. Email 3 (Day 14): graceful close. For each email note: subject angle, core message, and CTA. This plan is used by the writing agents and does not appear in the sent emails.

agents.name: Subject Lines
agents.systemPrompt: You write subject lines for a 3-email follow-up sequence. Each subject line must be different — no 'Re:' thread hijacking on email 2 or 3. Email 1: add a new hook. Email 2: add value. Email 3: close the loop gracefully. Each under 6 words. Return all three on separate lines, labeled 'Email 1:', 'Email 2:', 'Email 3:'.

agents.name: Sequence Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write 3-email cold follow-up sequences. Each email is distinct and stands alone — no 'just following up', no 'bumping this to the top of your inbox'. Email 1 (Day 3): reframe the original angle, shorter than the original. Email 2 (Day 7): add genuine value — a resource, insight, or case study that is useful regardless of whether they buy. Email 3 (Day 14): graceful close — give them an easy out while leaving the door open. All three under 80 words each. Format clearly labeled.

name: LinkedIn Connection Message
description: A LinkedIn connection request message and initial DM. A planner maps the approach (scratch), then body agents write both messages.

variables.label: Reason for Connecting
variables.exampleValue: Saw your post about scaling B2B sales teams — we work in the same space
variables.hint: Specific, genuine reason — reference a post, shared interest, or mutual context

variables.label: Who You Are (Brief)
variables.exampleValue: I run a sales enablement startup serving mid-market SaaS companies
variables.hint: One line about you — optional but helps legitimacy

agents.name: Outreach Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan LinkedIn outreach before any messages are written. Your plan covers: connection note angle (specific, under 300 chars), DM angle after acceptance (value or low-stakes ask), and what to avoid (pitching, generic openers). This plan is used by the writing agents and does not appear in the sent messages.

agents.name: Connection Request Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write LinkedIn connection request notes. LinkedIn connection requests are under 300 characters. The best ones are specific (reference something real), not salesy, and feel like one person talking to another. No 'I'd love to add you to my network.' Return only the connection request note.

agents.name: Initial DM Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write LinkedIn DMs sent after a connection is accepted. The best LinkedIn DMs are: short (under 80 words), reference the specific reason you connected, share something of value or make a low-stakes ask, and end with one clear question. They are never a pitch deck in text form. No 'I noticed we're both in the same industry.' Sign off with only your first name.
